text: We report here a standardized technique of the realization of a neovessi robotized intracorporeal on the Studer technique, taking a distance of fifty-five centimeters from the distal lobe, with an afferent axis of twenty centimeters to the retro-ideal anastomosis, an afferent axis of forty centimeters in length, including a chimney of fifteen centimeters. The retro-ideal anastomosis is performed before tubularization, at twenty centimeters from the distal extremity. Lens insulation is made in order to leave a good distance between the distal extremity and the cecum in order to facilitate an ileo-ileal estomac. The ileal section is made using a GIA-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A- to allow the realization of the chimney for uretero-ileal anastomoses. Digestive continuity restoration is carried out by a lateral-lateral ileo-ileal anastomosis, mechanical by the application of an endo-GA-XX clip, doubled to lengthen the length of the anastomosis, of an endo-GA-XXXV clip. The anastomosis is then closed by the application of an endo-GA-XX clip carrying all fragments of doubtful vitality. The urethral neo is created on the most mobile portion towards the urethra. The ideal urethral anastomosis is then carried out to stabilize the neovessi on the urethra. This also allows to expose the two legs of the neovessi.

The surgeon performs the second surgery of QUILI-TROIS-ZERO, focusing on reducing traction on the anastomosis to prevent rupture of the urethra or intestinal loops. The robot is redocked to adjust the angle, and perineal pushes are performed. The proximal extremity of the chimney is positioned in contact with the ureters for future anastomosis. The detubularization of the intestinal segment begins from the distal portion, progressing towards the urethra, leaving a centimeter-wide segment of intestinal tissue at the urethral anastomosis site.

The surgeon begins the posterior suture using Quiltra 3-0 sutures, progressively forming a posterior plate. The ideal urethral anastomosis is performed using Wallace's technique, with the option of Nesbitt's technique. The ureters are fixed to the abdominal wall to enhance exposure for the anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the ureters laterally and performs a uretero-ureteral anastomosis using a continuous suture of PDS 5-0, progressing from the bottom upwards. Urethral probes of CH-VIII caliber are intubated into the anastomoses, guided by terrumen guides, and are systematically fixed to prevent movement.

The surgeon exteriorizes the extremities of the hematoma through the neovessi, followed by performing the uretero-ileal anastomosis using two sutures. The first suture is placed on the anterior face, restoring uretero-ileal continuity.

The surgeon performs the plication of the neobladder to form a circular structure with good compliance. The lateral right extremity of the neobladder is positioned to the left of the retro-ileal anastomosis. Two continuous sutures are applied horizontally from the right side of the neobladder, progressing upwards towards the upper right end, using two Quiltra 3-0 sutures.

The surgeon performs the second suture, starting from the lower stop point and gradually rising to the left edge of the neovessi. The neovessi is then closed, followed by a waterproof test to ensure the integrity of the reconstruction.
